guest_role: General Partner at Benchmark (boards of Twitter, Cockroach, Optimizely, New Relic, Zendesk)
assertiveness_why: Harry plants a mild counter-thesis on valuation discipline ('surely some rigidity has to be applied to ensure fund-returning economics') and references outside commentary (Elad Gil's cycles piece) to frame questions, but otherwise lets Fenton's long answers run uncontested.
question_style: Long, well-researched questions that reference outside sources and prior Fenton interviews/quotes, occasionally posing a mild counter-argument for the guest to address (valuation discipline vs. 'never turn down on valuation'); no interruptions, mostly deferential once the guest starts answering.
tone: Highly reverential ('such a special individual', 'man crush on Peter'), formal-admiring register, minimal self-deprecation, clearly well-prepared from deep research into the guest's prior interviews.
depth: Substantively strategic: venture cycle theory, market-sizing philosophy for pre-paradigm products (Snapchat, Zenly), valuation vs. ownership-stake tradeoffs, and a detailed personal account of evolving as a board member (Socratic method, pre-reads vs. PowerPoint).
harry_quotes: How do you view the potential end of this tech cycle that we're currently in?
How do you look to balance that wonderful optimism with an element of caution?
Which founders made you feel the most uneasy and why?
learnings: Fenton resists mapping investments onto macro trends, arguing standout companies (Google in 1999, Snapchat) succeed by defying the perceived end of a cycle rather than confirming it.
He deliberately avoids prescribing product strategy early on, instead pressure-testing whether the founding team keeps returning to the company's authentic underlying purpose.
In his view, valuation rarely determines venture returns in hindsight; ownership stake and conviction in full potential matter far more than entry price discipline.
His early years as a board member were poor because he over-communicated strong opinions; mentorship pushed him toward a Socratic, question-driven board style.
He now pushes portfolio CEOs toward prose-based board pre-reads instead of PowerPoint decks so meeting time goes to dialogue rather than one-way presentation.
notable: Fenton candidly narrates his own evolution from a poor, opinion-dominant board member to a Socratic-method practitioner, a rare piece of self-critical VC introspection for this era of the show.
